source.title: BSEE Accident Investigation Report - Dropped 22" Casing

actions_taken: Drilling operations shut down on March 25, 2015 due to increasing surface currents
Rig held position and waited for currents to subside
Controlled drift to the north initiated at approximately 0.5 knots
Remotely Operated Vehicle (ROV) launched to locate dropped casing
Metallurgical testing performed on WHRT equipment
Discussions between BSEE and Shell ongoing to develop recovery plan
event.summary: On March 26, 2015, while performing drilling operations on the Noble Jim Day in Walker Ridge Block 508, approximately 3,107 feet of 22" casing was dropped to the seafloor. The rig was pre-running the 22" casing in an attempt to minimize downtime. Unforeseen loop currents increased from the forecasted 2-3 knots to 4.9 knots, causing the rig to lose dynamic positioning capability. A controlled drift was initiated to reduce loads on engines, thrusters, and the suspended casing string. At 09:00 on March 26th, uncommon rig movement indicated mechanical failure. Investigation revealed the Wellhead Running Tool (WHRT) Mandrel had snapped in two within an O-Ring groove due to excessive force from the casing weight combined with high loop currents. No subsea infrastructure or rig equipment was damaged. Metallurgical testing showed no equipment anomalies.

threats.name: Excessive Mechanical Stress on Wellhead Running Tool

controls.name: Weather Forecast Review
controls.barrier_role: Environmental assessment prior to casing operations

supporting_text: The extended weather forecast on the rig called for currents to be between 2-3 knots during the week prior to the incident
Over the next day, the loop currents continued to increase

controls.name: Operational Shutdown Due to Adverse Conditions
controls.barrier_role: Halt operations when environmental conditions exceed safe limits

supporting_text: On March 25, 2015, all drilling operations on the rig were shut down due to the increasing surface currents that were now ranging from 3.5 to 4.5 knots

controls.name: Controlled Drift Maneuver
controls.barrier_role: Reduce mechanical loads on suspended casing and rig systems during high current conditions

supporting_text: After it was determined safe to do so, the rig began a controlled drift to the north at approximately 0.5 knots. By using a controlled drift the rig was able to decrease the loads to the engines and thrusters as well as on the suspended string of casing
On the morning of March 26th at approximately 09:00, the controlled drift was stopped when the marine crew felt an uncommon rig movement, indicating a mechanical failure

controls.name: Wellhead Running Tool (WHRT) Design and Integrity
controls.barrier_role: Mechanical integrity of tool to support suspended casing load

supporting_text: Further investigation into the incident showed that the failure point occurred within one of the O-Ring grooves of the Wellhead Running Tool (WHRT) Mandrel, were it had snapped in two
it was determined that the failure of the tool was due to the excessive force from the weight of the casing along with the high loop currents experienced at the time of the incident

controls.name: Remotely Operated Vehicle (ROV) Inspection
controls.barrier_role: Verify subsea infrastructure integrity and locate dropped equipment

supporting_text: The Remotely Operated Vehicle (ROV) on the rig was launched in an attempt to locate the dropped casing and ensure that no subsea infrastructure had been damaged as a result of the incident. None of the equipment on the rig or on the seafloor was damaged due to the dropped casing

notes.rules: JSON output only. mentioned fields must be evidence-based. Use null for unknown values.
